text: WHO Pregnancy, Childbirth, Postpartum and Newborn Care: A guide for essential practice

This guide provides evidence-based recommendations for skilled care during pregnancy, labour, delivery, and the postpartum period. The guidance presented here is based on systematic reviews of the evidence and expert consensus, and should be adapted to local contexts and resources.
Chapter 3: Active Management of Third Stage of Labour

The third stage of labour is defined as the period from delivery of the baby until delivery of the placenta. Active management of the third stage of labour (AMTSL) has been shown to reduce the risk of postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) by approximately 60% compared to expectant management.
3.1 Key Components of AMTSL

items: Administration of a uterotonic agent (oxytocin preferred) within 1 minute of delivery of the baby, before delivery of the placenta
Controlled cord traction (CCT) with counter-suprapubic pressure to deliver the placenta
Uterine massage after delivery of the placenta

text: The timing of uterotonic administration is critical. Oxytocin 10 IU should be given intramuscularly immediately after delivery of the baby. If intravenous access is available and PPH is anticipated, oxytocin may be given as an infusion.
3.2 Oxytocin Use in AMTSL

caption: Oxytocin Administration for AMTSL and PPH Prevention/Treatment
rows: Within 1 min of baby delivery, before placental delivery
Use if IV access established and high risk

text: Oxytocin must never be given as a rapid intravenous bolus, as this can cause severe hypotension, cardiac arrhythmias, and circulatory collapse. Always administer by intramuscular injection or as a slow intravenous infusion.

The antidiuretic effect of oxytocin means that excessive volumes of hypotonic fluids should be avoided to prevent water intoxication and hyponatraemia, particularly with prolonged infusions. Careful fluid balance monitoring is essential.
3.3 Controlled Cord Traction

Controlled cord traction (CCT) is performed by applying gentle, steady traction on the umbilical cord while providing counter-traction with the hand suprapubically. The precise technique is critical to avoid cord breakage and retained placenta.

items: Support the uterus with the hand above the symphysis pubis (counter-traction)
Apply gentle, steady traction on the cord, avoiding jerky movements
Do not apply excessive force — if resistance is felt, wait for another contraction
Stop if the cord tears and allow the next uterine contraction to deliver the placenta
The normal timing for placental delivery is within 5-10 minutes

text: If the placenta is not delivered within 15 minutes of active management, this is defined as retained placenta and requires further management, potentially including manual removal under anaesthesia.
3.4 Uterine Massage

After delivery of the placenta, the uterus should be massaged to promote contraction and reduce bleeding. Place your hand on the abdomen and massage the uterus in a circular motion. A well-contracted uterus feels firm and hard, similar to a clenched fist.

Uterine massage should be continued for at least 2-3 minutes. In facilities with oxytocin, continued oxytocin infusion or further intramuscular doses are preferred to massage alone, but massage should still be performed to ensure adequate contraction.
Chapter 4: Prevention and Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage
4.1 Risk Factors for PPH

items: Multiparity (more than 4 previous pregnancies)
Prolonged labour
Induced labour (particularly with prostaglandins)
General anaesthesia
Placental abnormalities (abruption, previa, accreta)
Intrauterine infection/chorioamnionitis
Coagulopathy (known or suspected)
Severe anaemia
Obesity

text: All healthcare facilities should identify women at high risk of PPH during antenatal care. These women should deliver in facilities where emergency blood transfusion is immediately available.
4.2 Blood Transfusion in PPH

caption: Blood Loss and Transfusion Decision-Making in PPH
rows: IV fluids, oxytocin, observe vital signs
IV fluids, oxytocin, ergot alkaloids if available, crossmatch blood
Yes, if available and Hb <100 g/L
Large-bore IV (2 lines), aggressive fluid resuscitation, all uterotonic agents, urgent transfer if not in hospital
Yes, urgent — use O-negative if type unknown
Activate massive transfusion protocol if available, prepare for surgery, involve senior staff
Yes, urgent transfusion with blood products + ICU-level care

text: In settings where blood transfusion is not available, prevention of PPH through active management of the third stage of labour and early recognition of warning signs is critical.
Chapter 5: Management of Severe Complications
5.1 Eclampsia and Severe Pre-eclampsia

Eclampsia is characterized by seizures occurring in the context of hypertension and proteinuria in pregnancy or the postpartum period. Seizures should be treated immediately with parenteral anticonvulsants.

caption: First-Line Anticonvulsant Therapy for Eclampsia
rows: Alternate route; more painful but effective
If neither MgSO4 nor diazepam available; risk of toxicity

text: After control of seizures, maintain MgSO4 for 12-24 hours postpartum. Careful monitoring for signs of MgSO4 toxicity is essential, particularly in settings with limited renal monitoring. Serum magnesium levels should be maintained between 2-4 mmol/L.
Chapter 6: Antenatal Care
6.1 Minimum Antenatal Care Package

All pregnant women should receive a minimum of 4 antenatal care (ANC) visits: one in the first trimester, one in the second trimester, and at least two in the third trimester. Each visit should include assessment of maternal and fetal well-being.
6.2 Iron and Folic Acid Supplementation in Pregnancy

caption: Iron and Folic Acid Supplementation Recommendations for Pregnant Women
rows: High-dose folic acid (if history of NTD)

text: In populations with high anaemia prevalence (Hb <110 g/L in second trimester), routine iron supplementation should be offered to all pregnant women to prevent severe anaemia and reduce the risk of PPH.
6.3 Tetanus Vaccination in Pregnancy

All pregnant women should receive tetanus toxoid vaccination if they have not previously completed a primary immunization series. Tetanus is safe in pregnancy and is critical for prevention of neonatal tetanus.

items: If no prior tetanus immunization: Give 2 doses of tetanus toxoid 4 weeks apart (dose 1 in first visit, dose 2 in follow-up visit)
If 1 prior dose of tetanus: Give 1 booster dose (to complete 3 doses)
If 2 or more prior tetanus doses: Give 1 booster dose if more than 10 years since last dose

text: Chapter 7: Detection and Management of Labour Complications
7.1 Partograph Use in Labour Monitoring

The partograph is a simple graphical tool for monitoring labour progress and detecting abnormalities early. It combines cervical dilation curve, uterine contractions, vital signs, urine output, and molding of fetal skull into a single visual record.

items: Cervical dilation is plotted hourly on the graph; crossing the 'alert line' indicates slow progress
The 'action line' (4 hours ahead of the alert line) triggers urgent intervention (augmentation, transfer, or preparation for operative delivery)
Descent of the fetal head is similarly tracked
Uterine contractions are recorded as number and duration in 10-minute blocks

text: 7.2 Diagnosis and Management of Prolonged Labour

Prolonged labour is defined as labour lasting more than 12 hours in a nulliparous woman or more than 18 hours in a multiparous woman. Prolonged labour is associated with increased maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality.

caption: Management of Prolonged Labour by Stage and Parity
rows: Check cervical findings; assess for false labour
Rest, hydration, analgesia; re-assess in 2-4 hours
Oxytocin augmentation (if no CPD); assess fetal well-being
No progress after 2-3 hours (nullip) or 1 hour (multip)
Operative delivery indicated; prepare for vacuum/forceps or C-section
